content:
I came to dev.to to translate. I stayed to steal.

I mean that as the compliment it is here. On this site, "I'm stealing that line" is something you say to an author's face and they thank you for it. Ideas are meant to be lifted, reused, carried home. It took me a while to understand that culture — because I didn't arrive as a thief. I arrived as a tourist who couldn't read the signs.

Here's the setup. I'm Korean. My English is workable but slow, and writing a comment good enough to earn a real reply from a stranger — in a second language, in a technical field I never trained in — is more than I can do alone at a speed I'd tolerate. So when I started reading and commenting here, I opened an AI beside me and used it the obvious way: as a translator. Read the post, get the gist, draft a reply, fix my English, post it.

That was the whole plan. It lasted about a week.

The moment the tool changed jobs

What broke the plan was that the posts were good. Not content-farm good — actually good. People writing honestly about the thing that broke at 3am, the assumption that quietly rotted, the fix they were embarrassed they'd missed. I'd come for a translation and I'd leave with an idea lodged in my head that had nothing to do with the words.

At some point — I don't remember deciding it — I stopped asking my AI to just translate the post, and started asking it something else: "Is there anything in here we should actually be using?"

That question changed what the tool was. A translator turns one language into another. What I'd started doing was turning someone else's hard-won lesson into a change in my own system — and the AI wasn't a dictionary for that job. It was the thing that read the post, understood my setup, found the overlap, and then — the part that still surprises me — built it and tested it.

That's the theft this series is named for. Not the words. The lessons.

From translator to research partner, in three steps

Looking back, the tool climbed through three jobs, and I never noticed the ladder while I was on it.

One: translate. Turn the post into something I can read; turn my reply into something a native speaker won't wince at. Pure language. This is where everyone starts and where most people stop.

Two: mine. Read the post against my own situation and surface the part I can use. Not "summarize this" — "I run a small pile of internal tools held together with duct tape and good intentions; what in this post is load-bearing for me?" That reframing is the entire jump. The post stops being content and becomes raw material.

Three: apply. Take the mined idea and actually build it — write the code, run it against the real system, report back whether it worked, fix it when it didn't. Not a suggestion filed away for someday. A change that's live before the conversation ends.

I'm a physical therapist, not an engineer. I could never have done step three alone at the speed step two produces ideas. The AI closed that gap: read, extract, ship — one continuous motion instead of three separate skills I don't fully have.

The twist: the barrier was the point

Here's the part I keep turning over.

If my English had been fluent, I'd have used AI less, and gotten less out of dev.to. I'd have read the good posts, nodded, and scrolled on — the way you do with things that are easy. Because I needed the AI in the loop just to read, it was already there: already holding the context of my systems, already one sentence away from "so should we build this?" The friction I resented turned out to be the on-ramp.

The language barrier didn't make me a worse participant here. It made me a deeper user of the tool, because it forced the tool into the room for every single post. What looked like a handicap was the reason the whole pipeline existed at all.

What dev.to actually became

Somewhere in there, this site quietly changed categories for me.

I didn't come to broadcast, and I didn't stay to network, though both happen. What dev.to became is closer to a shared brain I get to borrow from — a place where a stranger debugs a firewall failure so I don't have to, where someone names the exact advantage of building outside a tech hub, where a person who lost 23 days to a silently-dead monitor writes it all down so I can close the same hole in an afternoon. Collective intelligence stops being a slogan when you're pulling working changes out of it every week.

And the AI is what makes it reachable across a language I only half-speak — not because it translates the words, but because it translates the lesson into my system.

So that's the series. Each post, I'll show you the receipts: a specific line from a real dev.to post, and the specific thing it became in my tools, usually the same week I read it. Some of them you'll recognize — because you might have written them.

Consider yourselves robbed. Thank you for leaving the door open.

This is the start of Stolen from the Feed — what a non-developer carries home from dev.to and actually builds. Sister series to From Zero to Ship.
